items_en: 1. Takes note of the report of the Secretary-General; 10

Welcomes the reaffirmation by Governments of their strong commitment to the further implementation of the Copenhagen Declaration on Social Development and the Programme of Action of the World Summit for Social Development, 1 inter alia, to eradicate poverty, promote full and productive employment and decent work for all and promote social integration in order to build stable, safe and just societies for all;

Recognizes that the implementation of the Copenhagen commitments and the achievement of the internationally agreed development goals, including the Millennium Development Goals, are mutually reinforcing and that the Copenhagen commitments are central to a coherent and people-centred approach to development;

6. Stresses the importance of sufficient policy space for Governments, in particular in the area of social spending and social protection programmes, and calls upon international financial institutions and donors to assist developing countries in achieving social development, in accordance with their national priorities and strategies, including through debt relief;

7. Recognizes that the comprehensive concept of social development affirmed at the World Summit for Social Development and the twenty-fourth special session of the General Assembly has not been fully taken into account in the formulation of national and international policies and that, while poverty eradication is at the heart of development-related action and discourse, greater emphasis should be placed on other commitments made at the Summit, in particular those related to employment and decent work and social integration, which have also been affected by the general disconnection of economic and social policies in the formulation of public policies;

items_en: 9. Recognizes that, during the first Decade, the implementation of the commitments made by Governments has not met expectations, and welcomes the proclamation, in its resolution 62/205 of 19 December 2007, of the Second United Nations Decade for the Eradication of Poverty (2008-2017), in order to promote, in an efficient and coordinated manner, the achievement of the internationally agreed development goals related to poverty eradication, including the Millennium Development Goals;

10. Stresses that the major United Nations conferences and summits, including the Millennium Summit, the 2005 World Summit, its own high-level plenary meeting on the Millennium Development Goals, the 2013 special event on the Millennium Development Goals, the International Conference on Financing for Development, the Monterrey Consensus of the International Conference on Financing for Development and the United Nations Conference on Sustainable Development, held in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil, from 20 to 22 June 2012, emphasized the priority and urgency of poverty eradication in United Nations development efforts;

11. Also emphasizes that policies to eradicate poverty must address both the phenomenon, its root and structural causes and its manifestations, and that equity, inclusiveness, reduction of inequality and empowerment of the poor must be included;

12. Reaffirms that each country is primarily responsible for its own economic and social development and that the role of national development policies and strategies cannot be overemphasized, and stresses the importance of taking effective measures, including, where appropriate, the establishment of new financial mechanisms, in support of the efforts of developing countries to achieve sustained economic growth and sustainable development, poverty eradication and the strengthening of their democratic systems;

13. Stresses that equity and social development presuppose the existence of favourable conditions and that, while economic growth is indispensable, the persistence of inequality and marginalization impedes broad-based and sustained growth, which is essential for inclusive, sustainable and people-centred development, and recognizes the need to ensure the balance and complementarity of growth measures and measures of justice and economic and social inclusion if they are to affect the overall level of poverty;

14. Also stresses that stable global financial systems and corporate accountability, as well as national economic policies that affect other stakeholders, are essential for creating an enabling international environment for economic growth and social development;

15. Recognizes the need to promote respect for all human rights and fundamental freedoms in order to respond to the most pressing social needs of those living in poverty, including through the design and implementation of human rights instruments,

items_en: 16. Reaffirms its commitment to gender equality and the empowerment of women, as well as to mainstreaming a gender perspective in all development activities, recognizing that these are essential elements for achieving sustainable development, combating hunger and malnutrition, poverty and disease, and strengthening policies and programmes that promote, ensure and expand the full and equal participation of women in all aspects of political, economic, social and cultural life, and to improve their access to all resources necessary for the full enjoyment of all their individual rights and fundamental freedoms by eliminating remaining obstacles, including in terms of equal access to full and productive employment and decent work for all, and to strengthening their economic independence;

17. Encourages Governments to promote genuine popular participation in civic, social, economic and political activities and in the planning and implementation of policies and strategies for social integration so as to facilitate the achievement of the goals of poverty eradication, full employment, decent work for all and social integration;

18. Reiterates its commitment to promote full-time, freely chosen and productive employment opportunities, including for the most disadvantaged, as well as decent work for all, including respect for fundamental principles and rights in the workplace, reaffirms the urgent need to create, at the national and international levels, conditions conducive to full and productive employment and decent work for all as a basis for sustainable development, that an enabling environment for investment, growth and entrepreneurship is essential for the creation of employment opportunities and that human resources development strategies should be based on national development goals that establish a strong link between education, health, training and employment, contribute to maintaining the productivity and competitiveness of the labour force and meet the needs of the economy, and reaffirms also the need for men and women to find productive work in a manner that respects freedom, equity, security and human dignity in order to succeed in eradicating hunger and inclusiveness in achieving economic growth and social well-being, and that it is essential for all countries to achieve full and sustained social development;

19. Stresses the importance of removing obstacles to the realization of the right of peoples to self-determination, which undermine their social and economic development, including by excluding them from labour markets, in particular in the case of peoples living under colonial or other forms of alien domination or occupation;

20. items_en: 21. Requests the funds, programmes and organizations of the United Nations system to integrate the goal of full and productive employment and decent work for all into their policies, programmes and activities and to support measures taken by Member States to achieve that goal, and invites financial institutions to support efforts in this regard;

22. Recognizes that, in order to promote full employment and decent work for all, investment in the education, training and skills development of women and men, as well as girls and boys, the strengthening of social and health protection systems and the implementation of international labour standards is also necessary, and urges States and, as appropriate, relevant United Nations entities and international and regional organizations, within their respective mandates, as well as civil society, the private sector, employers' organizations, trade unions, the media and other relevant actors, to continue to formulate and develop policies, strategies and programmes to enhance, in particular, the employability of women and young people and to enable them to achieve full and productive employment and decent work for all, including by promoting their access to formal and non-formal education, vocational training and development, continuing training, retraining and tele-education, inter alia, in the fields of information and communications technology and enterprise management, in particular in developing countries, including through the strengthening of their economic capacities;

23. Also recognizes that full and productive employment and decent work for all, including social protection, fundamental principles and rights in the workplace, tripartism and social dialogue, are key elements of sustainable development for all countries and therefore an important objective of international cooperation, and encourages innovative solutions in the development and implementation of employment policies and programmes for all, including the long-term unemployed;

24. Calls upon States to design and implement policies and strategies for the eradication of poverty, full employment and decent work for all, including the creation of productive and adequately remunerated employment, as well as social integration policies and strategies that promote gender equality and the empowerment of women and address the special needs of social groups such as youth, persons with disabilities and older persons, migrants and indigenous peoples, taking into account the concerns of those groups in the planning, implementation and evaluation of development programmes and policies;

25. Stresses the need to allocate adequate resources for the elimination of all forms of discrimination against women in the workplace, including unequal access to the labour market and wages, as well as for the elimination of discrimination against women in the workplace.

items_en: 33. Recognizes that the social integration of those living in poverty requires consideration of their basic needs, including nutrition, health, water, sanitation, housing and access to education and employment, in order to address them through integrated development strategies;

34. Reaffirms that social integration policies should aim at reducing inequalities, promoting access to basic social services, education for all and health care, eliminating discrimination, increasing the participation and integration of different social groups, in particular youth, older persons and persons with disabilities, and overcoming obstacles to social development resulting from globalization and market-driven reforms, so that everyone throughout the world can benefit from globalization;

35. Urges Governments to develop, in cooperation with relevant entities, social protection schemes to promote participation in the labour market and to combat inequalities and social exclusion and, as appropriate, to increase their effectiveness or reach, including for workers in the informal sector, invites the International Labour Organization to strengthen its social protection strategies and policies related to the extension of social coverage, and urges Governments to give priority, while taking into account national circumstances, to the needs of those living in poverty and those exposed to it and to pay particular attention to universal access to basic social protection systems, including the establishment of minimum social protection, which can provide a systemic basis for combating poverty and vulnerability, and takes note in this regard of the recommendation of the International Labour Organization on social protection floors;
36. Requests the United Nations system to continue to support the efforts of Member States at the national level to achieve inclusive social development through a coherent and coordinated approach;

37. Reaffirms the commitment to promote the rights of indigenous peoples in the fields of education, employment, housing, sanitation, health and social protection, and notes the inclusion of these issues in the United Nations Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples;

38. Recognizes the need to design social development policies as a clear and coherent whole, with the participation of those concerned, and recognizing that poverty is a multidimensional phenomenon, advocates, in this regard, joint public policies, and stresses the need to integrate them into a comprehensive strategy for development and well-being;
39. Recognizes the role that the public sector can play as an employer and its importance in creating effective conditions for full and productive employment and decent work for all;

40. Also recognizes the vital role that the private sector can play in terms of new investment, job creation and financing for development, in support of measures to ensure full employment and decent work for all, and encourages the private sector, including small and medium-sized enterprises and cooperatives, to contribute to decent work for all and to the creation of jobs for women and men, in particular for women and men.
